text: Today we'll compare a $10 steak from Walmart to a $300 steak from Japan. Those of
you that know me know that we're already quite familiar with Wagyu. We have a beautiful
piece of Australian Wagyu. Hit it with some salt. Smash. We're cooking. We had way
more Wagyu than we could possibly finish. I have probably eaten cows in my lifetime.
And I mean what's not to love? But many of you have raised the question: Is it worth
getting something like this when you come by 30 steaks for the same price? I'm as
you smack that subscribe button. All right, we're in the car and we are now pulling
up to the Walmart where we're going to be looking for the best bang for our buck for our steak.
All right. So for choosing our meat, we want to stick to that $10 mark. I do see down
here that I can get this nice-looking, it's got pretty good marbling here, USDA-choice
New York strip for about $10, but at the same time, we can also get this ribeye for
just $1 more and I think I'm going to do it. For a little under $12, the marbling
on this is actually quite nice and we're not going to match the wagyu by any means
but this could end up being a really, really good steak if we treat it properly.
We're back from Walmart with our $10 steak. Yes, I know, technically, it's $12 but
before we cook, I want to compare these two steaks. Now, naturally, the marbling
on our Wagyu is going to be nicer than the marbling from our Walmart steak here.
However, for the $12 we paid for this, I'm actually really happy with the amount
of fat content and marbling that we have. They've also left on some nice extra fat
on the edges that we can choose to use and render off.
Or trim before cooking. We'll begin by seasoning up both steaks with some good old
salt. Be sure to get both sides of each steak to make sure they're evenly and fully
covered and roll those edges around on the cutting board. Now our wagyu here is fully
seasoned and ready to be cooked. We don't want anything other than salt because we
don't want to mess with the flavor. Now for our ribeye here, we're going to have
some fun with it. We'll continue our ribeye by putting on a nice blend of fresh cracked
pepper, which I'll also go all around the edges with as well. And I'll finish with
a tiny pinch of garlic powder and a light sprinkle of smoked paprika. That right
there is a well-seasoned steak but I'm also going to make a delicious spring chimichurri
to go with it. For our chimichurri, I'll start by toasting off some corn. I almost
looked at this video as a challenge. Can I turn this steak into a masterpiece? I'll
char my corn just long enough that I get that nice charred flavor and color on the
outside but not too long because I want to keep my corn nice and crisp and crunchy. Personally, I like my corn raw.
It's raw!
Then into a blender, we'll add in some parsley, then we'll crack some garlic, and
in we go. Now a sprinkle of dried oregano, a few splashes of red wine vinegar, and
a nice splash of olive oil. We'll finish with a pinch of salt. Once we've blended
our chimichurri, I'll slice all of my corn, taking all of those beautiful bits off the edge.
Lil' bits.
And into my chimichurri, I go. Now we'll let our chimichurri rest in the fridge and
it's time to cook our meat. Because I want to let this rest for a bit longer, let's
start with our $10 Walmart ribeye. Since we have all this fat here, I want to start
by rendering that off. We don't want to want any of that fat to go to waste. Once
we've let that fat melt into the pan we lay it down to get that nice sear and make
sure to press down so all the steak gets that even crust. Now that right there is what we call a nice crust.
Nice crust, my boy.
We've gotten some darker, more charred spots on this steak and that's because we put
things like garlic powder, which are going to burn when you cook it in this high
heat. But sometimes people do that char on purpose and it tastes fantastic. At this
point, it's time to let our steak take a little nap.
Now the time has come to cook our beautiful Wagyu.
You're going to notice a lot more fat coming off this piece of meat which is exactly
what we're going to expect here. The smells coming off the wagyu are completely different.
With our ribeye, we had a lot of different flavors from the herbs and aromatics that
we added, whereas this is going to be much more beefy and robust. After just about
a minute or so, it's already time for a flip. I can promise you this: you're going
to have a hard time finding a better sear on a piece of wagyu like this. With our
wagyu complete, we'll set it off to the side to rest as well. The cooking process
is complete and both steaks look totally different, and quite honestly, equally appetizing.
Our $10 ribeye seems to have more juices running off of it.
Juicing.
Although that may be because the fat on here is just a little bit thicker and is retained
mostly in the meat as well as running slower off the plate. The crust we got on our
wagyu is a little bit lighter and perhaps a nicer-looking golden brown than our ribeye
here. Although for spring and summer grilling, this is the exact look you're going
for. After we've let it rest, it's time to slice into our ribeye and as always, it's
time for the moment of truth. Sometimes it can be hard to see on camera, but this
right here is a dead-on medium rare. You can also touch your finger on the inside
and if it's nice and warm, your steak is good to go.

What you're really looking for is that perfect marbling and that you didn't overcook
